bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: Angular Feature Folders — Colocation, Barrel Exports, and Path Aliases Done Right
bytes.body:
Feature-based folder structure in Angular groups a feature's component, service, model, pipe, and spec files into a single directory — the opposite of the type-based layout Angular CLI scaffolds by default.

A `users/` feature folder contains `user-list.component.ts`, `user.service.ts`, `user.model.ts`, and an `index.ts` barrel. The barrel defines the feature's public API: only explicitly exported symbols are accessible to the rest of the app. Consumers import from `@app/users`, never from deep internal paths like `../../users/user-list.component`.

Colocation makes refactoring cheap: moving or renaming a feature is a single folder operation. Angular's standalone component model (v15+) amplifies this — each feature manages its own dependency graph via `imports: []`, eliminating the `SharedModule` antipattern entirely.

Wire up path aliases in `tsconfig.json` to make barrel imports refactor-safe and IDE-navigable. Angular Language Service respects these aliases, so go-to-definition still works across the boundary.

Keep `index.ts` intentionally narrow — export only what other features need. Everything else stays private to the slice.

bytes.topic: Component Design — single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: Angular Component Design — Single Responsibility, the 150-Line Rule, and Splitting Smart from Dumb
bytes.body: Angular components balloon fast when you mix data-fetching, state management, and template logic in one class. The 150-line rule is a forcing function: if your component file exceeds it, extract. The pattern that pays most dividends is the container/presentational split — a 'smart' component owns the `@ngrxStore` selects, `HttpClient` calls, and event handlers, while a 'dumb' component receives `@Input()` data and emits `@Output()` events, nothing more. Dumb components are pure by nature: given the same inputs, they render identically. This makes them trivially testable with `ComponentFixture` and `NO_ERRORS_SCHEMA`, and freely reusable across routes. Apply `ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` to every presentational component — Angular will skip change detection unless an `@Input()` reference changes, a bound `async` pipe emits, or you manually call `markForCheck()`. For logic that doesn't belong in either layer — mapping API DTOs to view models, sorting, filtering — push it into a pure `@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })` service or a standalone pipe. Components should orchestrate, not compute.

bytes.topic: State Management — local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: Angular State Management — Local, Lifted, Service, and Store: When to Use Each
bytes.body:
Angular gives you four tiers of state management, and picking the wrong tier is the most common architecture mistake.

**Local state** (`signal()` inside a component) handles ephemeral UI — dropdown open, tab index, form dirty flag. Never reach outside the component for this.

**Lifted state** passes a signal or plain value via `@Input()` with change notification via `output()` (Angular 17+) or `EventEmitter`. Use this when two siblings share state through a common parent — no service needed yet.

**Service state** is an injectable with `providedIn: 'root'` (or scoped to a route) holding `signal()` or `BehaviorSubject`. Right for cross-component state that doesn't need time-travel or DevTools — user preferences, auth status, a paginated list.

**External store** (`@ngrx/signals` SignalStore or classic NgRx) adds action logs, selectors, and effects. Justify the boilerplate only when you need reproducible state, complex async flows, or team-scale predictability.

The signal APIs in Angular 16+ blur the lines — a well-typed injectable with `signal()` + `computed()` covers most "store" needs without NgRx overhead.

bytes.topic: Performance — memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: Angular computed() and memo strategies — When They Help vs. Hurt
bytes.body:
Angular's signals API (v17+) brings `computed()` as the primary memoization primitive. Unlike React's `useMemo`, `computed()` is fully reactive — it re-evaluates only when its signal dependencies change, tracked automatically at runtime. Use it for expensive derivations: filtered lists, aggregated totals, transformed data. Avoid it for trivial reads like `signal() + 1` — the tracking overhead exceeds the benefit.

For template expressions, Angular's change detection already skips re-evaluation in OnPush components when inputs haven't changed. Wrapping a cheap getter in `computed()` inside an OnPush component is redundant — the component won't re-render anyway.

The real win is cross-component sharing: a `computed()` defined in a service runs once per signal change regardless of how many components inject it. Contrast this with a plain getter in a service, which re-executes on every read.

Avoid chaining many `computed()` calls deeply — Angular evaluates the dependency graph lazily, but excessive nesting increases graph traversal cost. Flatten where possible.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: Angular Data Fetching — Server State vs Client State with TanStack Query
bytes.body:
Angular developers often conflate server state and client state — stuffing both into NgRx or a BehaviorSubject service. They're fundamentally different: server state is async, owned remotely, and can go stale; client state is synchronous, owned by your app, and always fresh.

TanStack Query's Angular adapter (@tanstack/angular-query-experimental) handles server state precisely. Use injectQuery() to declare a query bound to a key — it automatically caches, deduplicates, and background-refetches. Pair it with Angular signals: query.data() is a signal, so your template reacts without manual subscriptions or AsyncPipe gymnastics.

Client state — selected tab, modal open/closed, form draft — still belongs in component signals or a lightweight signal-based service. Don't route UI state through QueryClient.

Configure staleTime per query: 0 for real-time data, 60_000 for near-static lookups. Use placeholderData: keepPreviousData to avoid layout thrash on pagination. Call queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['users'] }) inside injectMutation's onSuccess to sync server and cache atomically — no manual refetch wiring needed.

bytes.topic: Forms — controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: Angular Forms — Reactive vs Template-Driven, and Wiring Zod for Schema Validation
bytes.body:
Angular offers two form strategies: template-driven (uncontrolled-style — `ngModel` drives state in the template) and reactive (controlled-style — `FormGroup`/`FormControl` live in the component class). For validation-heavy forms, reactive wins. Angular 14's strictly-typed reactive forms (`FormGroup<{email: FormControl<string>}>`) catch field-name typos and shape mismatches at compile time.

To replicate the React Hook Form + Zod pattern, wire a Zod schema directly into Angular's validator pipeline. Write a `zodValidator()` factory that calls `schema.safeParse(ctrl.value)` and maps failures to `ValidationErrors`. Pass per-field schema slices via `schema.shape.email` — this co-locates validation rules with the schema, keeps the component class free of `if/else` guard logic, and gives you typed error objects renderable in templates via `control.errors?.['zod']`.

Template-driven forms still fit simple UIs with minimal validation — avoid reactive boilerplate when two-way `ngModel` binding is sufficient.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: Angular Component Inputs — Discriminated Unions Over Loose Props
bytes.body:
Angular 17.1+ input() signals make prop typing even more critical. Avoid `any` or bloated optional-everything interfaces by modeling variants as discriminated unions on your `@Input()` or `input()` declarations.

Consider a `<app-badge>` that renders differently for 'status', 'count', and 'label' modes. A single interface with every field optional forces defensive checks everywhere. Instead, define a union type where each member has a literal `type` discriminant — TypeScript's control-flow narrowing then makes the wrong fields invisible in each branch.

Inside the component, inject the `BadgeProps` via `input.required<BadgeProps>()`. In the template, Angular's `@switch` on `props().type` narrows correctly: in the `'count'` branch, `props().value` is typed as `number`, not `number | string | undefined`.

This pattern eliminates runtime guards like `if (props.value !== undefined)`, catches misuse at the call site (`<app-badge [props]="{ type: 'count', label: 'x' }" />` won't compile), and makes the component's API self-documenting without a single JSDoc comment.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: Angular Error Handling — Route-Level Error Boundaries with ResolveFn and ErrorHandler
bytes.body:
Angular lacks React-style error boundaries, but you can approximate per-route isolation using a combination of `ResolveFn`, `CanActivateFn`, and a custom `ErrorHandler`. For loading/error/empty states, use signals in a service paired with `@if` (Angular 17+ control flow) in templates.

Define a `ResolveFn` that catches errors and returns a typed result object instead of throwing — this prevents the entire router outlet from collapsing. In the component, check the resolved value's `error` field and render accordingly.

For a global safety net, provide a custom `ErrorHandler` in `bootstrapApplication` that catches uncaught exceptions and navigates to an `/error` route. This mimics a top-level boundary.

Empty states are best handled as a distinct signal state (`'idle' | 'loading' | 'success' | 'empty' | 'error'`) rather than checking `data.length === 0` inline. This makes the template exhaustive and avoids null-check clutter. Combine with `@switch` for clean, readable state-driven rendering per route.

bytes.topic: Testing — test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: Angular Testing Library — Test What Users See, Not What Components Do
bytes.body:
Angular Testing Library (@testing-library/angular) flips the default TestBed mindset: instead of querying by component class or CSS selector, you query by what a real user perceives — visible text, ARIA roles, labels, and placeholders. This forces tests to survive refactors that don't change behaviour.

With vanilla TestBed you might do `fixture.debugElement.query(By.css('.submit-btn'))`. That breaks the moment you rename a class. With Testing Library, `screen.getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` survives any styling change because it mirrors how assistive technology navigates your UI.

The philosophy also discourages testing internal state. Don't assert on `component.isLoading`; assert on `screen.getByRole('status')` or `screen.queryByText('Loading...')`. Fire events with `userEvent.click()` rather than calling component methods directly — it dispatches the full browser event chain, including `focusin` and `blur`, catching bugs that `triggerEventHandler('click')` silently misses.

Result: tests that document real user journeys, fail only on real regressions, and double as living accessibility audits.

bytes.topic: Security — dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: Angular Security: innerHTML Bypasses, Token Storage, and DomSanitizer
bytes.body:
Angular's template engine auto-escapes interpolation (`{{ }}`), but `[innerHTML]` bindings are sanitized — not escaped. Angular strips dangerous tags like `<script>` and event handlers automatically via its built-in sanitizer. The trap is `bypassSecurityTrustHtml()` from `DomSanitizer`: calling it tells Angular to skip sanitization entirely, trusting your string as safe HTML. Only use it when rendering known-safe, server-controlled markup — never on user-supplied content.

For token storage, avoid `localStorage` for auth tokens — it's accessible to any JS on the page, making it XSS-vulnerable. Prefer `httpOnly` cookies (set server-side), which are inaccessible to JavaScript entirely. If you must use memory storage, keep tokens in a service-level variable, not a global.

For form inputs, Angular's `ReactiveFormsModule` doesn't sanitize values — that's your job. Validate with Validators and strip or encode before sending to APIs. Never interpolate raw form values into dynamic queries or HTML. Angular's `HttpClient` auto-encodes URL params, but body payloads are your responsibility.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: Angular Code Quality — @angular-eslint Rules, Naming Conventions, and the 50-Line Method Rule
bytes.body:
Angular's `@angular-eslint` package brings component-aware static analysis that generic ESLint rules miss. The `component-selector` and `directive-selector` rules enforce Angular's style guide at lint time: components must use element selectors with a kebab-case prefix (`app-user-card`), while directives use attribute-style camelCase (`appHighlight`). Violations surface as CI failures instead of silent runtime mismatches.

`@angular-eslint/no-empty-lifecycle-method` removes noise from scaffolded stubs — empty `ngOnInit()` or `ngOnDestroy()` bodies add reviewer overhead and signal incomplete implementations. Pair it with `@angular-eslint/use-lifecycle-interface`: Angular requires explicit interface declarations (`implements OnInit`) to catch hook typos at compile time. Without the interface, a misspelled `ngOnlnit()` silently never runs.

For the 50-line rule, add `max-lines-per-function` at 50 to your ESLint config. OnPush components frequently bloat inside `ngOnInit` with combined data fetching, subscription wiring, and state initialization. When the rule fires, it's a signal to extract fetching into a dedicated `load()` private method or push initialization logic into a route resolver — keeping each lifecycle hook to a single, auditable responsibility.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: Angular Accessibility — Semantic HTML First, ARIA as Last Resort
bytes.body: Angular developers often reach for ARIA attributes prematurely. Start with semantic HTML — `<button>` over `<div (click)>`, `<nav>` over `<div class="nav">`. Angular's CDK provides `FocusTrap` and `LiveAnnouncer` for programmatic focus management that screen readers respect. Use `cdkTrapFocus` directive to contain focus inside modals, and inject `LiveAnnouncer` to announce dynamic content changes (e.g., after an HTTP call resolves). Only layer ARIA when native HTML semantics fall short — for example, a custom `ComboboxComponent` built from `<input>` + `<ul>` needs `role="combobox"`, `aria-expanded`, and `aria-activedescendant` because no native element maps to that pattern. Bind these dynamically with Angular's property binding (`[attr.aria-expanded]="isOpen"`), never hardcode them in the template. For route changes, Angular Router doesn't move focus automatically — use `Router.events` filtered to `NavigationEnd` to manually focus a skip-link or `<h1>` via `ViewChild` and `nativeElement.focus()`. Test with `axe-core` integrated into your Karma or Playwright suite.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: Angular i18n — Stop Hardcoding Strings, Use $localize and Built-in Pipes
bytes.body:
Angular's built-in `$localize` tagged template literal is the idiomatic way to mark strings for extraction — never inline raw text in templates or TypeScript. Running `ng extract-i18n` generates an XLIFF/XMB file translators consume. At build time, Angular compiles locale-specific bundles, eliminating runtime lookup overhead entirely.

For dates, numbers, currencies, and percentages, Angular's pipes (`DatePipe`, `CurrencyPipe`, `DecimalPipe`, `PercentPipe`) delegate to the Intl API internally. Register the locale data you need with `registerLocaleData`, provide `LOCALE_ID` at the application level, and the pipes pick it up automatically — no manual `Intl.DateTimeFormat` calls needed.

For runtime locale-switching without rebuilding (common in SPAs), use `ngx-translate`: inject `TranslateService`, call `use('de')` on preference change, and bind with `{{ 'KEY' | translate }}`. Keep keys namespaced (`dashboard.header.title`) rather than using raw sentences — copy changes break sentence-as-key schemes. Never co-locate translated strings directly in component TypeScript; all user-facing text belongs in your XLIFF file or translation JSON, making audits and handoffs reliable.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: Angular Web Vitals — How Component Patterns Break LCP, CLS, and INP
bytes.body:
Angular's component decisions have a direct line to your Core Web Vitals scores.

**LCP:** Use the `NgOptimizedImage` directive (`[ngSrc]`) — it auto-generates `srcset`, sets `fetchpriority="high"` when you add `priority`, and enforces `width`/`height` attributes. Never wrap above-the-fold images in `@defer`; deferred blocks sit behind a JS-triggered fetch boundary and will crater LCP.

**CLS:** `@if` blocks that toggle after an async resolve are CLS traps. The DOM insertion shifts surrounding content unless you pre-reserve space with `min-height` on the container, or render an unconditional skeleton. `ViewContainerRef.createComponent()` dynamic insertions carry the same risk — size the host element before the component lands.

**INP:** Angular's default `ChangeDetectionStrategy.Default` runs a full Zone.js dirty-check on every DOM event. Switching to `OnPush` + Angular Signals narrows detection to explicit signal writes. For scroll or resize handlers that never mutate view state, call `NgZone.runOutsideAngular()` — this removes the event entirely from Zone tracking and routinely cuts INP by 40–60 ms on event-dense components.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: Angular Memory Leaks — ngOnDestroy, AbortController, and takeUntilDestroyed
bytes.body:
Angular components that subscribe to Observables, attach DOM listeners, or fire HTTP requests without cleanup are memory leak factories. Every active subscription holds a reference to the component, preventing garbage collection long after the view is destroyed.

The classic fix is implementing OnDestroy and calling unsubscribe() inside a Subject-based pattern — but Angular 16+ ships takeUntilDestroyed(), a DestroyRef-backed operator that handles this automatically, even inside inject() calls outside the component class.

For native DOM listeners added via addEventListener, you must call removeEventListener in ngOnDestroy with the exact same function reference — anonymous functions won't match. Store the handler as a class property.

For in-flight HTTP requests, Angular's HttpClient doesn't auto-cancel on destroy. Pair takeUntilDestroyed() with your HTTP stream, or manually wire an AbortController signal via HttpClient's context/fetch API if you need native cancellation semantics.

Avoid manual unsubscribe() arrays — they're error-prone. Prefer the reactive pattern: one Subject + takeUntil, or the modern inject(DestroyRef) + takeUntilDestroyed() combo.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: Angular WebSockets — Singleton Connection, Reconnection, and Optimistic UI
bytes.body:
Angular's dependency injection makes WebSocket management clean: provide a single service at the root level so every component shares one connection. Use RxJS `webSocket()` from `rxjs/webSocket` — it returns a `WebSocketSubject` that handles serialization automatically. For reconnection, pipe through `retryWhen` (or `retry({ delay: ... })` in RxJS 7+) combined with `timer` to implement exponential backoff without manual interval management.

For optimistic UI on message send: update your local `BehaviorSubject` state immediately before calling `socket$.next(payload)`. If the server ACKs with a correlation ID, reconcile — otherwise roll back on error using `catchError`. This keeps the UI snappy without waiting on round-trip latency.

Avoid creating `new WebSocket()` directly inside components — that spawns duplicate connections on re-render or route change. The `providedIn: 'root'` singleton survives navigation and reconnects transparently. Expose the stream as an `Observable` from the service; components only subscribe, never hold the subject reference.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: Angular Environment Config — Validate with Zod at Bootstrap, Never at Runtime
bytes.body:
Angular's built-in `environment.ts` / `environment.prod.ts` files — swapped via `fileReplacements` in `angular.json` — give you build-time config but carry zero validation. A mistyped key or missing `apiUrl` silently becomes `undefined`, surfacing only when an HTTP call fails in production.

Pair them with Zod: define a schema, call `EnvSchema.parse(environment)` at module initialisation, and bind the validated result to an `InjectionToken<AppEnv>`. Because `parse()` throws synchronously on failure, the error surfaces at bootstrap — not in a 3 AM incident.

Provide `APP_ENV` with `useValue` (not `useFactory`) so validation executes before any service constructor fires. In services and standalone components, inject with `inject(APP_ENV)` for a fully typed object — no more `environment.apiUrl!` non-null assertions or defensive `?.` chains. Swap the token in unit tests via `{ provide: APP_ENV, useValue: mockEnv }` without touching real config.

For Angular SSR, add a second Zod schema over `process.env` in `server.ts` for the same startup guarantee on the Node side.

bytes.topic: API Layer — single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: Angular HTTP Layer — One HttpClient, Interceptors, Zero Raw Fetch
bytes.body:
Angular's `HttpClient` is a singleton provided by `provideHttpClient()` — inject it once per service, never directly in components. Components own UI state; services own data fetching. Violating this makes interceptors blind to your requests.

Interceptors are where cross-cutting concerns live. Register them via `withInterceptors([authInterceptor, retryInterceptor])` (functional style, Angular 15+). An auth interceptor clones the outgoing request and attaches a Bearer token; a retry interceptor uses RxJS `retry({ count: 3, delay: 1000 })` to handle transient 5xx errors without duplicating that logic across 40 services.

Never call `fetch()` or `axios` in Angular — you lose the interceptor chain, zone.js change detection integration, and `HttpClientTestingModule` mock support. The `HttpContext` API lets interceptors communicate per-request (e.g. skip auth on public endpoints) without polluting headers. Keep interceptors pure and composable: one concern per interceptor, ordered intentionally at registration.

bytes.topic: Animation — CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: Angular Animations — CSS for Simple States, @angular/animations for Complex, prefers-reduced-motion Always
bytes.body:
Reach for plain CSS transitions first. Toggling a class with a host binding or [class.active] covers 80% of UI state changes — hover, focus, open/closed — without touching Angular's animation engine at all.

For sequenced, staggered, or state-machine animations, use @angular/animations. Wrap the app with BrowserAnimationsModule (or provideAnimationsAsync() in standalone), then define triggers with trigger(), state(), style(), and transition() directly on the component. Angular wires the DOM updates to animation lifecycle so you never fight change detection.

The part most teams skip: respecting prefers-reduced-motion. Inject the BreakpointObserver from @angular/cdk/layout and observe '(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)'. When true, swap your AnimationMetadata array for a zero-duration NoopAnimationsModule alias or simply pass [] to the trigger — no CSS overrides needed. Wire this at bootstrap with provideAnimations() vs provideNoopAnimations() based on the user's OS setting so the entire app honors the preference without component-level guards.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: Angular Global UI Patterns — Toast, Modal, and Confirm as Injectable Services
bytes.body:
Angular's global UI primitives — toasts, modals, confirmation dialogs — belong in singleton services, not scattered across component trees via `*ngIf` flags or `@ViewChild` hacks in `AppComponent`.

For toasts, use `ApplicationRef.attachView()` with `createComponent()` (Angular 14+) to mount a `ToastComponent` directly onto `document.body`. The component owns its own timer and calls `ApplicationRef.detachView()` on teardown — no parent needed.

For modals and confirm dialogs, Angular CDK `Overlay` is the idiomatic solution. Inject `Overlay`, call `overlay.create({ hasBackdrop: true })` to get an `OverlayRef`, then attach via `ComponentPortal`. Pass data and a result channel using a custom `Injector` built with `Injector.create()`. Expose a clean `open(message): Observable<boolean>` API from the service — callers subscribe and react declaratively.

This pattern keeps `AppComponent` pristine, makes dialogs testable in isolation, and avoids the `z-index` wars that come from portal-less approaches. All three patterns compose: a confirm dialog can itself trigger a toast on resolution.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: Angular Bundle Optimization — Tree-Shaking, Lazy Routes, and Bundle Analysis
bytes.body: Angular's build pipeline (esbuild via `@angular/build`) performs tree-shaking automatically, but you can sabotage it by importing entire modules. Import only what you need: `import { MatButtonModule } from '@angular/material/button'` instead of all of `MatModule`. For heavy components — rich text editors, chart libraries, PDF viewers — use route-level lazy loading via `loadComponent()` in your route config. This defers the chunk until navigation, keeping the initial bundle lean. For library-heavy features, combine lazy routes with `loadChildren()` pointing to a standalone routes array. To audit what's bloating your bundle, run `ng build --stats-json` and feed `dist/stats.json` into `webpack-bundle-analyzer` (still works with esbuild output via `esbuild-bundle-analyzer`). Look for duplicate dependencies, unexpectedly large chunks, and eagerly loaded modules that should be lazy. Set `budgets` in `angular.json` to enforce size limits in CI — builds fail if a bundle exceeds your threshold, preventing regressions from sneaking in.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: Angular Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, and tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.body:
Angular projects ship with ESLint via `ng add @angular-eslint/schematics`, but formatting and pre-commit enforcement require extra wiring. Add Prettier alongside it, then configure Husky + lint-staged to gate every commit: lint-staged runs `eslint --fix` and `prettier --write` only on staged `.ts` and `.html` files — critical for Angular's two-file component pattern where template linting is separate from TS linting.

In CI, never rely on `ng build` alone to catch type errors — it skips checking lazy-loaded modules by default. Run `tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.app.json` explicitly to surface type errors across the full project graph, including standalone components and signal-based inputs introduced in Angular 17+.

Pair this with `ng lint` in your GitHub Actions workflow as a separate step. The combination — Prettier on commit, ESLint on commit, `tsc --noEmit` + `ng lint` in CI — creates a layered gate that catches formatting drift, template binding errors, and type regressions before they reach main.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .lintstagedrc.json
{
  "*.ts": ["eslint --fix", "prettier --write"],
  "*.html": ["prettier --write", "eslint --fix"]
}

# GitHub Actions step (ci.yml)
- name: Type check
  run: npx tsc --noEmit -p tsconfig.app.json

- name: Lint
  run: npx ng lint

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: Angular Production Monitoring — Sentry, Web Vitals, and Source Maps
bytes.body:
Angular's build pipeline makes production debugging surprisingly clean when wired correctly. Install `@sentry/angular` and initialize it in `main.ts` before `bootstrapApplication`, passing `dsn`, `environment`, and `release` (match your CI build hash). Register `SentryErrorHandler` as Angular's `ErrorHandler` provider and add `TraceService` to catch route-level performance traces via the Angular Router integration.

For Web Vitals, use the `web-vitals` package inside `APP_INITIALIZER` — report `onCLS`, `onLCP`, `onINP`, and `onFID` to Sentry via `Sentry.captureEvent` with a `measurement` type, or pipe them to your analytics endpoint. This hooks into Angular's app lifecycle cleanly.

Source maps are the critical piece. In `angular.json`, set `sourceMap: { scripts: true, hidden: true }` under the production configuration — `hidden` prevents browsers from loading maps publicly while still letting Sentry symbolicate. Upload maps during CI using `sentry-cli releases files <release> upload-sourcemaps ./dist/<app-name>/browser` after `ng build --configuration production`.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models — how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: Angular's Reactivity vs React, Vue, Svelte & Solid — What Zone.js and Signals Actually Do
bytes.body:
Angular's default change detection runs a full dirty-check of the component tree after every async event — clicks, HTTP responses, timers — because zone.js monkey-patches these APIs. This is closest to React's virtual DOM model: both re-evaluate broadly, then reconcile differences.

Vue's Proxy-based reactivity and Svelte's compile-time subscriptions are more surgical — only components that consumed a reactive value re-run. Angular Signals (stable since v17) bring the same fine-grained model, mirroring Solid's push-based approach. A signal() update propagates only to computed() and effect() that directly read it — not the whole tree.

Switch to ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush plus Signals and you escape zone.js entirely: no more unnecessary CD cycles in parent components, no markForCheck() boilerplate. The Angular team is progressively making Signals the default primitives — EXPERIMENTAL_provideZonelessChangeDetection lets you opt out of zone.js today for fully reactive, pull-free applications. Every other framework already lives here; Angular now gives you the on-ramp.
